segments.s195-g33: سپه را: 'سپه' (army) is marked by the object/topic marker 'را' and is fronted for emphasis ('to/for the army'). 'همه ترگ و جوشن' is a quantifier phrase 'all rags and (all) cuirasses/armor' with 'همه' modifying the coordinated nouns. بداد is an archaic past-tense verb (3rd singular) corresponding to دادن 'to give' (here 'he gave' / 'bestowed') and appears at the end of the clause, completing the SOV-like classical Persian order (objects/phrases precede the verb).
segments.s195-g34: یکی introduces an indefinite subject 'one / a certain (person)' (here 'one Roman'). ترگ is the (direct) object 'a rag', رومی is the noun 'Roman' identifying the subject, بسر is an adverbial phrase 'on the head' (classical placement: prepositional/adverbial element before the verb) and برنهاد is the past-tense verb 'he placed/put on' (برنهاد = 'raised/placed upon'). Word order is typical older Persian: subject (یکی رومی) + object (ترگ ... بسر) + verb (برنهاد).
segments.s195-g35: میانرا is a locative phrase 'the midst / in the middle' with the object/definite marker 'را' (here marking the place). بخفتان is an archaic/poetic verbal form used adverbially (its exact meaning is uncertain in the modern lexicon); رومی is the (direct) object 'the Roman', and ببست is a past-tense verb (3rd singular) meaning 'he bound / tied' (here taking رومی as its object). The clause shows fronted locative ('میانرا') and object before the verb, consistent with older Persian syntax.
segments.s195-g36: بیامد is a past tense verb 'he came' (3rd singular). 'کمان کیانی بدست' is a noun phrase 'the Kayanian (royal) bow in (his) hand' where کیانی is an adjectival/attributive modifier 'Kayanian, royal' and بدست is a phrase 'in hand / at hand' placed after the noun — thus the clause means 'he came with the Kayanian bow in hand.' The subject is omitted (understood from previous clauses).
segments.s195-g25: مراگر is an archaic conditional particle meaning “if to me / if for me”; it introduces the condition. زمانه is the topical subject meaning “fate / the world” and شدست is a perfect/past form “has become / has come to be” (here functioning as the verbal nucleus). اسپری is a noun placed after the verb phrase as the complement/predicate (literary word-order: condition — fate has made me a ...).
segments.s195-g26: This clause is an admonition: زمانه is the topic (“the world / fate”), ز is the preposition meaning “because of / from”, بخشش is the noun “giving / generosity” qualified by فزون “abundant”; نشمری is a negative exhortation/imperative from the verb شمر “to reckon / count” (i.e. “do not count / do not reckon”). Word order places the topic up front, then the qualifying prepositional phrase, and finally the negative verb.
segments.s195-g27: بروز is an adverbial prepositional expression “on the day / upon the occasion”; جوانی here marks the genitive/temporal phrase “of youth / in my youth.” پدر is the grammatical subject and کشته شد is a passive perfect construction: کشته (past participle, “killed”) plus شد as the auxiliary (“was killed”).
segments.s195-g28: مرا (here split in the data as م + را) is the dative/possessive/topic marker “to me / my” (مرا = ‘for me’), with را as the object/topic marker attached. روز is the noun “day.” چون introduces a simile “like / as,” so چون روز او = “like his day.” گشته شد is a compound perfect: گشته (perfect participle “become / turned”) plus شد as auxiliary, giving “has become.”
segments.s195-g29: بدست here functions as a locative/prepositional phrase “into the hand / into the power of.” گروی in this line is a literary/archaic word (here indicating seizure/captivity or a related notion) and combines with آمد “came” to express that something came into someone’s control. او را is the marked object/topic (“to him / his”), and زمان is the noun “time” placed after the verb as the topical subject (literary inversion).
segments.s195-g30: سوی is a preposition meaning “toward / to”; جان من is a possessive phrase “my life / my person” (جان used for one’s person or life); بیژن is the proper name (subject) and آمد is the verb “came.” دمان (دامان) here is an adverbial/nominal image “to the skirt/side” indicating arrival beside someone (literary idiom “to one’s side”). Word order is prepositional phrase → object → subject → verb → locative.
segments.s195-g31: بکوشم is a first-person singular present/subjunctive “I will strive / I shall try.” نمیرم is a negative present/future “I will not die” (here used idiomatically: ‘I shall not perish’). مگر introduces an exception/conditional “unless.” غرم‌وار is a poetic adverbial/qualifier (—وار suffix = “-like”) giving a manner: “in a ...-like way” (here a literary adjective/adverb modifying the implied dying).
segments.s195-g32: نخواهم is first-person negative “I will not wish / I will not ask.” ز is the preposition “from / of,” taking ایرانیان (“the Iranians”) as its object. زینهار is an interjection/admonition “beware / take heed” placed at the end as a warning addressed to the addressee(s). The clause thus states refusal to seek mercy from the Iranians, followed by the admonition.
segments.s195-g17: This is two coordinated predicates: 'رخش گشت پرخون' uses the past/copulative verb گشت ('became') after the noun رخش (here treated as the subject) to mean 'Rakhsh became bloodied.' The second part 'و دل پر ز دود' is a nominal phrase joined by و ('and') meaning '(and) (his) heart full of smoke' — 'پر' functions as the adjective 'full (of)' and 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of', so the whole phrase is a verbless descriptive clause (subject or circumstance carried over into the next clause).
segments.s195-g18: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (or 'he came'). به بالین is a prepositional phrase 'to/unto the bedside' (بالین used as 'by/bedside'), and the two following words form the proper name or epithet 'فرخ فرود' (here functioning as the person at whose bedside he comes). Word order is Subject omitted → verb → indirect object/goal phrase.
segments.s195-g19: بدو is the prepositional clitic meaning 'to him' (to that person) placed before the verb; گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said' (past). 'بیدار گرد‌ای پسر' is the reported imperative: بیدار 'awake' plus گرد (an archaic/poetic copula/auxiliary meaning 'become' used imperatively = 'become awake'), and ای marks the vocative 'O' addressing پسر ('son' / young man).
segments.s195-g20: که introduces the content clause/explanation ('for' / 'that'): 'ما را بد آمد' is an idiomatic past construction meaning 'ill happened to us' (بد آمد = an unfavorable event befell). ز اختر is an ablative 'from the star' (astrological omen). بسر here is archaic; in context it marks the sphere or impact ('upon us / to (our) head/lot') and functions adverbially with آمد.
segments.s195-g21: سراسر is an adverbial 'throughout' or 'all over,' modifying the following NP. 'همه کوه' = 'the whole mountain' (همه as 'whole/entire'), and پر + دشمنست expresses 'is full of enemies' — پر here is the predicate modifier 'full (of)' combined with the copula ending -ست attached to 'دشمن' to make 'is (with) enemies.'
segments.s195-g22: در دژ 'in the fortress' begins the locative clause. 'پر از نیزه و جوشنست' uses the prepositional phrase 'پر از' = 'full of' followed by the list 'نیزه و جوشن' (spears and armor). The final -ست on جوشن indicates the copula 'is' attached to the last coordinated noun, so the clause reads '(it) is full of spears and armor.'
segments.s195-g23: بمادر 'to (his) mother' is a prepositional phrase marking the addressee. چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت 'said'). 'جنگی فرود' is an epithet + name ('the warlike Farud') serving as the speaker; word order places the indirect object first, then the reporting formula and the speaker's epithet.
segments.s195-g24: که introduces the reported question/exclamation. 'از غم' = 'because of grief'; 'چه داری' is the present interrogative 'what do you have / what is in you' (چه 'what' + داری 2nd-person present of داشتن). 'دلت' is دل + 2nd-person enclitic ت 'your heart' (possessive suffix). The predicate 'پر ز دود' follows and describes the state: 'full of smoke' (پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of/from' + دود 'smoke').
segments.s195-g9: بخواب here is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in sleep' (contraction of به خواب) and sets the circumstance for دید. آتشی is used as a noun/adjectival substantive 'a burning/fire' (–ی marks indefinite/qualitative). دید is the simple past 'he saw'. کز is the relative/connector 'that/which'. دژ بلند follows in noun + adjective order meaning 'high fortress.' Word order: Circumstance (بخواب) + object (آتشی) + verb (دید) + relative (کز) + location (دژ بلند).
segments.s195-g10: برافروختی is a 2nd-person singular past form 'you kindled' (used in epic narrative to report an action attributed to 'you'). پیش means 'in the presence of / before' (location/relational). آن ارجمند = 'that esteemed/noble one' (آن = demonstrative 'that', ارجمند = 'esteemed'). The verb برافروختی links back to آتشی (the fire) as its action and the prepositional phrase پیش آن ارجمند marks the beneficiary/location.
segments.s195-g11: سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout, all over.' سپد here means 'whiteness/white' and modifies کوه ('white mountain' or 'mountain of white/gleam'); the phrase is emphatic. بفروختی is a past form but in this poetic context does not carry the ordinary sense 'you sold' — it functions as a transitive past verb meaning 'you made it blaze/caused it to...'(archaic/poetic usage). The literal order is adverbial (سراسر) + modifier (سپد) + noun (کوه) + verb (بفروختی).
segments.s195-g12: پرستنده 'the worshipper/devotee' and دژ 'the fortress' are coordinated by و. همی is an older continuous/aspectual particle giving an ongoing sense ('was/kept on'). سوختی here is interpreted as past/passive-inchoative 'was burning / burned up' in narration (classical forms can look like 2nd-person endings but function as 3rd-person narrative). The verb thus applies to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s195-g13: دلش = دل + ـش 'his heart'. گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' (here 'became'). پر + درد = 'full of pain' (پر is an adjective meaning 'full (of)'), so دلش گشت پر درد = 'his heart became full of pain'. و connects the clauses; بیدار گشت = 'he became awake' (bیدار + گشت). Word order: possessor-attached noun (دلش) + copular verb (گشت) + predicate (پر درد).
segments.s195-g14: روانش = روان + ـش 'his soul/spirit'. پر از درد = 'full of pain' where از functions as the 'of' preposition after پر. و تیمار = 'and care/anxiety' (تیمار = solicitude, care). گشت again is 'became'. The clause parallels the previous: possessive noun + predicate 'full of X' + copular verb.
segments.s195-g15: بباره is a verbal noun/archaic verb-form meaning 'to pour / it poured' or 'a downpour' (poetic). برآمد is the past 'arose/appeared'. جهان 'the world' is subject or scene-setting noun. بنگرید is a past/historical narrative form of نگر 'to look' (classical imperfect/optative shape used in narration) meaning 'he looked' or 'he cast his gaze'. The sequence describes a sudden event (rain/commotion) and the act of looking.
segments.s195-g16: همه 'all/the whole' modifies کوه 'mountain' = 'the whole mountain.' پرجوشن is a compound adjective 'full of mail/armored' (پرجوشن = 'covered in armor/mail'); و نیزه 'and spears' coordinates with that, and دید is the past 'he saw'. Typical classical word order places the object/scene before the verb (همه کوه ... دید = 'he saw the whole mountain ...').
segments.s195-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when” introducing a subordinate clause. خورشید تابنده شد is a nominal phrase with تابنده (shining) as an adjective modifying خورشید (sun); شد is the past-tense copula/verb ‘became’ linking the adjective to the subject. ناپدید follows as a predicative adjective (‘vanished’), so the whole segment reads “when the shining sun became vanished/hidden.”
segments.s195-g2: This is the main clause: شب تیره (the dark night) is the subject phrase, بر چرخ is a prepositional phrase “over/on the sky,” and لشکر کشید is a verbal phrase where کشید is the past of کشیدن ‘to draw/send/array’ meaning here ‘marshaled/drew (its) host.’ Word order is subject – adverbial – verb (typical Persian).
segments.s195-g3: دلیران دژدار is a stacked modifier sequence: دلیران (plural adjective/noun ‘the brave ones’) followed by دژدار (literally ‘fortress-holders’ or castellans) qualifies the following noun. مردی هزار places the numeral after the noun — an archaic/poetic ordering meaning ‘a thousand men’ (lit. ‘men, a thousand’).
segments.s195-g4: ز سوی کلات is a compound prepositional phrase: ز (poetic from) + سوی (side/direction) + کلات (the place-name Kalat or stronghold) = ‘from the direction/side of Kalat.’ اندر آمد is a two-word verbal unit where اندر is a locative/old-form preverb ‘in/into’ and آمد is the past tense ‘came.’ سوار at the end is an appositive/epithet meaning ‘mounted/horseman,’ describing those who came.
segments.s195-g5: در دژ (in the fortress) is a locative prepositional phrase. ببستند is the 3rd-person plural past ‘they closed/shut’ (here ‘they shut themselves in’). زین is literally ‘saddle’ and here likely part of a compacted/poetic phrasing indicating equipment or manner; روی تنگ — روی as ‘upon/on’ and تنگ ‘narrow/tight’ — together convey the idea of being closed up tightly or a narrow/close posture. The segment as a whole describes securing/locking up inside the fortress.
segments.s195-g6: خروش جرس خاست is a short clause where خروش جرس (‘the roar/clamor of the bell’) is the subject and خاست is the past-tense verb ‘arose/began.’ و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and,’ and آوای زنگ is a noun phrase ‘the voice/sound of the bell/ringing’ that functions as a second, linked nominal element describing the noise produced. The verb applies to both phrases: the bell-clamor arose, and the ringing’s sound (was heard).
segments.s195-g7: This segment names a locus or figure (جریره), then uses بتخت (a fused classical form meaning ‘on the throne’ or similar) plus the adjective گرامی ‘honored/esteemed,’ and بخفت is the past-tense verb ‘slept.’ The sequence is nominative phrase → locative/nominal phrase → predicate verb: ‘Jarīreh slept on the honored throne.’ Some words are archaic/poetic or uncertain in form, so the gloss reads as a conventional classical Persian formula.
segments.s195-g8: شب تیره (the dark night) is the subject; با (with) introduces the accompanying items درد و غم (pain and sorrow) as comitative or descriptive complements; بود is the past-tense copula ‘was.’ جفت here means ‘paired’ or ‘matched,’ so the clause says that the dark night was matched/paired with pain and sorrow (i.e. the night was full of pain and grief).

sentences.s195-s17: He gave the army all rags and armor; one Roman placed a rag on his head.
sentences.s195-s18: He bound the Roman in the midst and came with the Kayanian bow in his hand.
sentences.s195-s13: If, by fate, a steed has come to me, do not reckon the world to be abundant in generosity.
sentences.s195-s14: On the day of my youth my father was slain; my day has become like his day.
sentences.s195-s15: Time fell into captivity; Bijan came toward my life (to my side).
sentences.s195-s16: I will strive and will not die; unless I perish ignobly, I will not beg mercy from the Iranians — take heed.
sentences.s195-s9: Rakhsh became bloodied, and (his) heart filled with smoke; he came to the bedside of Farrokh Farud.
sentences.s195-s10: He said to him, 'Awake, O son, for an ill omen has befallen us from the star.'
sentences.s195-s11: Throughout, the whole mountain is full of enemies; the fortress is full of spears and armor.
sentences.s195-s12: The warlike Farud thus said to his mother: 'Because of grief, what do you have? Your heart is full of smoke.'
sentences.s195-s5: In his sleep he saw a fire that you kindled on the high fortress before that noble one.
sentences.s195-s6: Throughout the white mountain (lit. 'throughout the whiteness of the mountain') you caused it to blaze; the worshipper and the fortress were burning.
sentences.s195-s7: His heart became full of pain and he awakened; his spirit became full of pain and care.
sentences.s195-s8: Rain began to fall; the world arose — he looked and saw the whole mountain armored and spears.
sentences.s195-s1: When the shining sun became hidden, the dark night drew its host across the sky.
sentences.s195-s2: A thousand brave, fortress-holding horsemen came mounted from the direction of Kalat.
sentences.s195-s3: They shut themselves up in the fortress; a clamor of bells arose and the sound of ringing rang out.
sentences.s195-s4: Jarīreh (or the small place) slept upon the honored throne; the dark night was paired with pain and sorrow.
